name: Alta
short_description: High-altitude BLM rockhounding near Salt Lake City in the Wasatch Range foothills. Find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in rocky alpine terrain with stunning mountain views and free public acc...
description:
Alta sits in the Wasatch Range at the northern end of the Little Cottonwood Canyon area, approximately 30 minutes southeast of Salt Lake City. This BLM-managed public land offers excellent rockhounding opportunities in a classic high-elevation Utah mining district setting. The terrain features exposed granitic bedrock and weathered pegmatite formations that have attracted rockhounders and miners for generations.

The area is geologically part of the Wasatch Range batholith complex, a Precambrian to Paleozoic granitic intrusion that formed during multiple phases of mountain building. Pegmatitic dikes running through the granite contain well-formed crystals of quartz, feldspar (orthoclase), mica, and tourmaline. The high elevation and glaciated terrain have produced excellent crystal specimens that are exposed in talus fields, stream drainages, and rocky outcrops along the higher slopes.

Rockhounding at Alta rewards those willing to hike into alpine terrain. Specimen quality improves dramatically as you move away from the main parking areas. The combination of exposed bedrock, mineralized pegmatite, and natural weathering processes creates ideal conditions for finding quality quartz clusters, feldspar crystals, and tourmaline specimens. Many collectors focus on the talus slopes and wash areas where gravity naturally concentrates heavier mineral material.

As a popular ski resort area, Alta sees significant seasonal closure due to avalanche danger and deep snow. Winter recreation (skiing) is the primary use from November through April. Rockhounders should plan visits during the open season when backcountry access is safer and snow has cleared from lower elevations.

The remote location and high elevation mean proper preparation is essential. Cell service is spotty at best. Always carry adequate water, weather-appropriate clothing, and a map. The terrain transitions quickly from maintained resort areas to wild backcountry, so respect posted boundaries and avalanche closure signs even during the open season.
history: The Alta area sits within the Wasatch Range batholith, a massive Precambrian granitic intrusion that underlies much of the central Wasatch Mountains. Pegmatitic dikes associated with this batholith formed during the cooling and crystallization process, creating zones rich in large quartz, feldspar, and mica crystals. This geological setting has made the area a destination for both professional and amateur mineral collectors since the early 20th century.
tips: Focus your search on talus fields and hillside exposures above 9,000 feet elevation where pegmatite dikes are most concentrated and weathering naturally exposes fresh specimens. Bring a geology hammer or chisel to carefully extract crystals from the pegmatite matrix, and wear heavy gloves since the granite is extremely sharp and unforgiving. Look for areas where water runoff has washed away finer material, concentrating heavier mineral specimens in small drainages and wash bottoms. Early morning hikes allow you to cover the most ground before afternoon weather develops at this elevation.
terrain_notes: Alta sits at 8,500 to 10,500 feet elevation depending on location. Terrain is steep and rocky with exposed granitic bedrock, talus slopes, and alpine vegetation (low shrubs, wildflowers in summer, scattered conifers). Walking surfaces are uneven, loose rock is abundant, and the ground can be wet and muddy near snow melt areas in early summer. Most productive collecting requires hiking 0.5 to 2 miles from parking areas into steeper backcountry terrain.
best_season: June - September. Winter snow closes most backcountry access and creates avalanche hazards. Spring and early summer may have lingering snow patches above 9,000 feet. Fall is excellent but weather becomes unpredictable after mid-September. Summer heat at this elevation is mild, but afternoon thunderstorms are common.
written_directions: From Salt Lake City, head south on I-15 for approximately 15 miles. Take exit 41 toward Murray/Cottonwood Canyon. Follow signs for Little Cottonwood Canyon Road (UT-210) eastbound. Drive 7.5 miles up the canyon on UT-210 to Alta. The main resort parking area is at the end of the road. Additional BLM access points are available along UT-210 between miles 4 and 7 in the canyon. Pull off at signed BLM trailhead parking areas. Do not park on private ski resort property.
road_conditions: UT-210 (Little Cottonwood Canyon Road) is paved and well-maintained but steep with tight switchbacks. Passenger vehicles can access the canyon. Winter closures are frequent due to avalanche danger. Road can be icy in fall/spring. No high-clearance or 4WD required for paved canyon road, but backcountry access beyond parking areas requires hiking.
parking_notes: Limited parking at BLM pull-offs along UT-210 below the ski resort. Most trailhead parking is small (4-8 vehicle capacity). Main resort parking is private ski resort property and off-limits to rockhounders. Arrive early to secure roadside parking. During peak summer, parking can be difficult.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of large boulders or extensive digging. Collect from exposed material and talus only. Respect posted boundaries and avalanche closure signs. Pack out all waste. This is BLM public land - follow Leave No Trace principles.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No large-scale digging or blasting
Respect avalanche closure signs
Pack out all waste
Stay on designated trails where posted
nearest_services: Salt Lake City, 30 miles northwest. Sandy/Cottonwood Heights area near canyon entrance, 15 miles northwest. Limited services in canyon. No water, food, or fuel available at Alta itself.
accessibility_notes: Steep canyon drive with switchbacks and sharp curves. Backcountry rockhounding requires hiking on uneven, rocky terrain with significant elevation gain. Loose talus, steep slopes, and no established trails in many collecting areas. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy hiking boots essential. Significant fitness level required for productive collecting at high elevation. Not suitable for people with limited mobility.

faq.question: What crystals can I find rockhounding at Alta, Utah?
faq.answer: Alta is known for quartz specimens, feldspar crystals (especially orthoclase), mica sheets, and tourmaline. The area's pegmatitic granitic bedrock produces well-formed crystals, particularly in higher elevation talus fields above 9,000 feet. Many collectors find clear to white quartz clusters and black tourmaline specimens.

faq.question: Is there a permit or fee for rockhounding at Alta BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit or fee is required for personal use rockhounding on this BLM public land. Collection is free under FLPMA recreational use allowances, limited to 25 lbs per day per person. No commercial permits are issued for this location.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound near Alta, Salt Lake City?
faq.answer: June through September is optimal. Winter avalanche danger closes backcountry access, and spring often has lingering snow above 9,000 feet. Summer provides the most stable weather and accessible terrain, though afternoon thunderstorms are common. Early mornings offer the best conditions.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Alta for beginners?
faq.answer: Beginners can collect specimens along UT-210 and easier lower slopes, but Alta's real potential requires hiking into steep alpine terrain. The high elevation (8,500-10,500 feet), loose talus, and distance from parking make this better suited for intermediate to experienced rockhounders. Children should be 12+ and experienced hikers.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Alta, Utah?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer or chisel, heavy work gloves (granite is sharp), sturdy hiking boots, and a backpack. The high elevation demands extra water, weather-appropriate layers, sun protection, and a map. No motorized tools are permitted. A small to medium bucket helps carry specimens within your 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: Can I take my dog rockhounding at Alta?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM public land. However, Alta's steep terrain, high elevation, and distance from water make it challenging for dogs. Keep pets close to avoid wildlife encounters and avalanche-prone terrain. Bring extra water for your dog, and be prepared to carry a tired dog back to the vehicle.

meta_title: Alta Rockhounding - Utah BLM Quartz & Feldspar
meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and tourmaline at Alta, Utah. Free BLM public land rockhounding near Salt Lake City with stunning alpine terrain. No permit required.
